input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: 1/10 th‑scale autonomous vehicle retrofitted as a teleoperated test platform. The physical twin consists of a lower and upper chassis integrated with an advanced sensor suite and autonomous control electronics. It is configured to run Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) experiments, providing real‑time vehicle state data, projected trajectory and sensed object tracks. The model vehicle operates in a controlled test environment and is suitable for digital twinning due to its scalable hardware, clear separation between chassis layers, and the ability to off‑load computation to external devices.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin System (DTS) supplies three primary services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface that streams, archives and visualises real‑time vehicle state information, projected driving path and detected object tracks; (2) Computation Offloading – the DT hosts the ACC algorithm on a remote cloud or edge server, computing the desired speed from the lead‑vehicle distance and feeding the command back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set the desired target speed, or issue an emergency braking command from the dashboard. The services are accessible to operators (for monitoring and remote control) and developers (for troubleshooting, data collection and performance analysis).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin system is built upon a real‑time communication infrastructure capable of maintaining an end‑to‑end latency of at most 100 ms between the physical twin and the Digital Twin. Data acquisition modules continuously capture high‑rate telemetry from the vehicle’s advanced sensors. The collected data is persisted in a structured log (e.g., a relational storage backend) for audit and analysis. The operator and developer interfaces expose 3D visualizations of the vehicle’s pose and surrounding object tracks, support interactive reporting, and provide command interfaces for remote ACC management.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The DTS mandates sub‑100 ms clock synchronization accuracy (≈100 ms) for all underlying devices to enable computation off‑loading and satisfy safety‑critical vehicular standards. All Functional Entities (FEs) must maintain a common notion of time; the required accuracy is driven by hardware limitations. No explicit synchronization frequency or temporal granularity is specified beyond this 100 ms accuracy bound, though the system operates on that base for timestamp alignment of sensor data and control commands.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The DTS comprises three distinct entity instances: the Physical Twin Entity, the Digital Twin Entity, and the User Entity. Each entity is mapped to a separate deployment node and hosts one or more ROS2 packages corresponding to the Functional Entities (FEs). The Physical Twin Entity houses compute, sensor, and actuator resources and implements general automotive functions such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking; it also hosts the data pre‑processing FE and parts of device communication. The Digital Twin Entity manages data storage, data management, and control logic; the User Entity provides the User Interface FE, split into 3D Visualisation and Control Panel sub‑components. The architecture is effectively centralised from a functional perspective but physically decentralised across hardware nodes, with coordination achieved via ROS2 topics/services over a Wi‑Fi network.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The overall orchestration follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture. Functional Entities are realised as independent ROS2 packages; each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the required functionality. The Physical Twin Entity implements automotive‑specific and device‑control FEs; the Digital Twin Entity implements data‑management and control FEs; the User Entity implements UI‑centric FEs. Communication between entities is provided by the ROS2 middleware over a Wi‑Fi network, enabling point‑to‑point and publish‑subscribe exchanges of sensor data, control commands, and visualization streams. Resource allocation and deployment are explicitly decoupled by deploying each FE on its designated hardware component, thereby ensuring clear ownership of functionality while enabling coordinated operation of the full DTS.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin entity incorporates a multi‑layered computational model composed of: (1) an ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) behavior model implemented as a finite state machine that drives target speed and lateral position; (2) a geometric representation model reconstructing the ego vehicle and surrounding obstacles from LiDAR (Hokuyo UST‑10LX) and Intel D435i depth camera point‑clouds, processed with DBSCAN clustering and Kalman filter tracking; (3) an analytical report generation model that aggregates telemetry (speed, steering angle, object distances) and produces human‑readable diagnostic PDFs via a reporting functional entity. Data sources are collected by the Data Collection FE, transmitted over a time‑synchronised CAN‑Lite bus, stored in a PostgreSQL SQL database per Requirement R3, and served to the Digital Twin FE via RESTful JSON endpoints. The models are linked through shared data structures: the LiDAR and camera feed populate the geometric model; sensor data is fed into the ACC for control decisions; and all datasets are written to the database to enable traceability.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical twin’s actuation subsystem comprises a lower‑level chassis routing commands to the steering actuator and the Vedder electronic speed controller (ESC). The steering actuator is a servo motor providing ±30° rotation with 0.5° resolution, actuated through a PWM interface exposed over an OPC UA server. The ESC converts desired speed setpoints (0–30 m/s) into motor‑voltage commands (0–48 V) and reports back torque and RPM over an embedded CAN‑FD interface. Actuation is driven by the Acc Control FE, which receives user‑originated speed/throttle requests (via a WebSocket API) and applies feed‑forward/feedback loops consistent with Requirement R6. Safety constraints enforce a maximum acceleration of 2 m/s² and steering rate limits of 8°/s, with a watchdog timer that interrupts actuators if command packets are not received within 200 ms.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing architecture consists of a Hokuyo UST‑10LX 2D LiDAR (range 0.04–3.5 m, 10 Hz, ±0.5° angular resolution) and an Intel D435i depth camera (baseline 0.14 m, depth drift <1 cm over 3 m, 30 Hz). Additional vehicle‑state sensors provide wheel speed, steering angle, and battery voltage, sampled at 50 Hz over LIN. Data is streamed via a high‑bandwidth Ethernet link using the OPC UA binary protocol with Quality of Service (QoS) level 2. Point‑cloud pre‑processing uses DBSCAN clustering on the LiDAR stream and two‑dimensional Kalman filters on the depth camera slices, producing object centroids with a positional accuracy better than 3 cm. The aggregated dataset includes timestamped glyphs (ISO‑8601 UTC), correlation IDs for sensor fusion, and transmission latency metrics maintained within 5 ms.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin design mandates high‑fidelity realtime simulation, quantified by a maximum network latency of 100 ms (Requirement R7) and clock synchronization jitter less than 100 ms (Requirement R8). Validation is achieved through cross‑checking simulated object trajectories against ground truth from a lidar‑based map, with a maximum positional deviation of 0.1 m under nominal conditions. Uncertainty is captured by propagating measurement noise in the Kalman filter; confidence intervals (95%) are logged. An automated test harness injects synthetic faults into the sensor data stream to trigger error‑detection routines; discrepancies exceeding 0.2 m trigger an alarm and roll‑back to safe state. Quality assurance includes nightly regression tests on the data‑storage schema, CI pipelines for FE unit tests with 90 % code coverage, and a periodic audit of data integrity using checksums stored in the SQL database.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the vehicle’s physical twin—including LiDAR raw point clouds, vehicle state, motor and steering actuation signals, and servo feedback—is ingested by the Data Pre‑Processing FE. The raw LiDAR stream is first subjected to clustering and tracking by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component, producing object tracks and refined vehicle state information. All incoming sensor streams are then serialized, type‑aligned, and unit‑converted by the ISO‑23247 Data Translation FE. Communication among these software components occurs over an internal ROS 2 network (DDS‑based), while inter‑entity messaging (between the physical twin, the digital twin, and the user interface) travels over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP as the transport protocol. Real‑time updates are rendered on the 3D Visualization FE.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Data translation and service orchestration are performed within an internal ROS 2 network leveraging DDS for inter‑component communication. Inter‑entity communication—including messaging from the Digital Twin to the Physical Twin and user commands—is carried over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP. The document does not provide specifics about bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security mechanisms, suggesting a single‑hop Wi‑Fi topology linking sensors, processing nodes, and the user interface.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin supplies decision support for an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system by continuously monitoring vehicle dynamics, automatically determining the lead vehicle detected from sensor feeds, and computing a desired cruise speed based on current traffic spacing. A remote user interface embedded within the Digital Twin enables operators to view ACC status, access real‑time speed and spacing metrics, and issue remote control commands or override decisions.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system aligns with ISO 23247 Part 2 (Digital twin framework for manufacturing – reference architecture). It implements an entity‑based reference architecture comprising Data Collection and Device Control, Digital Twin, User and Observable Manufacturing Element entities, each realized through Functional Entities. The architecture may be instantiated with protocols such as OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as a concrete implementation platform.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin system adopts a multi‑layered safety and security strategy grounded in ISO 26262‑1:2018 and ISO 15622:2018.  ISO 26262‑1 defines the functional‑safety lifecycle – hazard analysis, risk assessment, ASIL determination, safety concept, and sustained verification and validation – which the system applies to its critical control modules (e.g., ACC logic).  ISO 15622:2018 specifies performance requirements for Adaptive Cruise Control, mandating fail‑safe operation, redundancy of sensor inputs, and tight latency bounds for safety‑critical loops.  Vehicle‑to‑everything (V2X) services are incorporated via 3GPP TS 22.185, which prescribes a security architecture that includes authentication (challenge‑response or X‑509 certificate exchange), key management (dynamic key provisioning), and message‑level encryption (e.g., AES‑128/256).  Access control is enforced through role‑based permissions on data exchange between the virtual cockpit and the vehicle domain, ensuring that only authorized entities can instantiate or modify digital‑twin models.  The model‑driven architecture described by Dalibor et al. enforces a strict separation between the virtual cockpit and the vehicle control layer, creating a safe boundary that isolates simulation errors from physical actuators.  Combined, these measures provide a comprehensive safety‑critical and cybersecurity posture for the Digital Twin system.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

It captures the specific functionalities (e.g., viewing reports, computing lead vehicle speed, emergency braking) and correctly identifies the user classes (operators and developers). Quotes: '1) Data Monitoring... view, save, and generate reports', '2) Computation Offloading... offloading only the ACC system', '3) Remote Management... enable/disable ACC'.

The description correctly identifies the infrastructure requirements, such as the 100ms latency threshold and the use of a SQL database for storage. It also covers visualization and command interfaces. However, it misses specific named technologies and frameworks explicitly mentioned in the text, such as ROS2, NVIDIA Jetpack, and the specific programming languages used (Python, C++). Quotes: 'maintain network latency of at most 100 ms', 'Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database', 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

It accurately describes the functional responsibilities and the physically decentralized but functionally centralized nature of the architecture. Quotes: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component' and 'Physical Twin Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general automotive functionality.'

The extraction accurately describes the orchestration following ISO 23247, the realization of FEs as ROS2 packages, and the communication architecture using ROS2 middleware over Wi-Fi. It correctly notes the decoupling of resource allocation and deployment to ensure clear ownership. Supported by quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase' and 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network.'

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the use of DBSCAN and Kalman filters for point cloud processing, as well as the existence of ACC, reporting, and data storage components, which are supported by Table I and Section IV. However, it adds extensive unsupported technical details, such as the specific database type 'PostgreSQL', communication protocols 'CAN-Lite' and 'RESTful JSON', report formats 'PDFs', and the specific implementation of ACC as a 'finite state machine'. None of these specifics appear in the source text. Supported quotes: 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter.'; 'Report Generation Generates human-readable reports...'.

While the extraction correctly names the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and references the 'lower level chassis' found in Section III-B, the vast majority of the description consists of specific technical parameters and protocols that are absent from the source. The source does not mention steering servo resolution (±30°, 0.5°), interfaces (PWM, OPC UA server, CAN-FD, WebSocket), voltage specs (0–48V), or safety constraints (watchdog, acceleration limits). Supported quotes: 'lower level chassis is responsible for controlling the steering and the speed... and a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller.'; 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands.'

The extraction accurately lists the sensor models (Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR and Intel Deepsense D435i) present in Section III-B and the algorithms (DBSCAN, Kalman) from Table I. However, it fills the description with highly specific performance metrics (e.g., range 0.04–3.5m, depth drift <1cm), protocols (Ethernet, OPC UA binary, LIN), and accuracy figures (3cm) that are not mentioned in the provided text. The source mentions 'Wi-Fi network' and 'ROS2' for communication, not the protocols listed in the extraction. Supported quotes: 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera'; 'Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter.'

The extraction correctly cites the network latency (100 ms) and clock synchronization (100 ms) requirements from R7 and R8. However, it heavily embellishes the description with validation and quality assurance processes that are not in the source, such as cross-checking against 'lidar-based maps', specific 'positional deviation' limits, 'automated test harnesses' for fault injection, and 'CI pipelines' with '90% code coverage'. These details are unsupported. Supported quotes: 'R7. The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms...'; 'R8. Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.'

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework

relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item

block_5_judge.reasoning: The source mentions design and operation phases briefly in the context of general automotive Digital Twins (Section II.B), but does not describe the specific lifecycle phases, representation types, transition management, or governance approaches for the Adaptive Cruise Control Digital Twin presented in the case study. The document focuses on architecture and implementation rather than lifecycle management.

While the document describes the specific steps taken in the case study (Requirements, Architecture, Implementation), it does not detail quality assurance practices, testing and validation approaches, version control, or evolution management strategies as required by the characteristic definition. The 'methodologies used' are limited to the case study approach itself rather than a generalizable engineering process.

The extraction is highly specific and well-supported by the text. It accurately captures the decision support capabilities described in Section III.A, including the ACC logic ('determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed') and the remote management features ('enable the management and operation of ACC remotely', 'sending an emergency braking command').

The extraction accurately summarizes the standardization details from Section II.A. It correctly identifies the 'ISO 23247 Part 2' reference architecture, lists the specific entities ('Data Collection and Device Control Entity', 'Digital Twin Entity', 'User Entity', 'Observable Manufacturing Element'), and notes the potential instantiation with 'OPC Unified Architecture' as mentioned in the text.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on system architecture, ISO 23247 standards, hardware components, and functional requirements for the Adaptive Cruise Control Digital Twin. It discusses data collection, storage, and monitoring but does not contain information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection, consent management, or data anonymization. Therefore, the assessment that the information is missing is correct.

The extraction contains extensive technical details and references that are not supported by the provided text. It claims the system uses ISO 26262-1:2018 and details specific security protocols from 3GPP TS 22.185 (e.g., AES-128/256, X.509 certificates), whereas the source text only references ISO 23247 and ISO 15622 (R6), and cites 3GPP 22.185 only for network latency requirements (R7).
